identity.name: Seal
identity.oneLiner: Decentralized secrets management with identity-based, threshold encryption and onchain access policies on Sui.

sources.quote: Seal enables identity-based encryption and decryption of sensitive data, with access controlled by onchain policies on Sui.
Seal is a decentralized secrets management (DSM) service that relies on access control policies defined and validated on [Sui](https://docs.sui.io/concepts/components).

entities.name: Seal
entities.description: Decentralized secrets management product securing data at rest via identity-based encryption with onchain Sui access policies.

sources.quote: Seal enables identity-based encryption and decryption of sensitive data, with access controlled by onchain policies on Sui.
Seal is a decentralized secrets management (DSM) service that relies on access control policies defined and validated on [Sui](https://docs.sui.io/concepts/components).

entities.name: Sui
entities.description: Layer-1 blockchain whose Move packages define and validate Seal's access control policies.

sources.quote: Seal leverages [Sui](https://docs.sui.io/concepts/components) for controlling access to the decryption keys
with access controlled by onchain policies on Sui

entities.name: Walrus
entities.description: Decentralized storage where Seal-encrypted data is stored at rest.

sources.quote: Application developers and users can use Seal to secure sensitive data at rest on decentralized storage like [Walrus](https://docs.wal.app/) or any other onchain / offchain storage.
Secure personal data on [Walrus](https://docs.wal.app/) or some other storage

entities.name: Mysten Labs
entities.description: Organization behind Seal, publishing the @mysten/seal SDK and operating seal.mystenlabs.com.

sources.quote: The [TypeScript SDK](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mysten/seal) is available through npm.
such as `seal.mystenlabs.com`

entities.name: Key server
entities.description: Lightweight off-chain service that validates onchain access policies and returns identity-based derived decryption keys.

sources.quote: A Seal key server is a simple and lightweight service that validates access control on Sui and generates identity-based private keys for authorized users to decrypt encrypted data.
Key servers are off-chain services that validate onchain access policies and return derived secret keys to authorized users.
Seal supports two server types for key management: **Decentralized** (committee mode) and **Independent**.

entities.name: Identity-Based Encryption (IBE)
entities.description: Cryptographic primitive Seal uses to encrypt data, with Boneh-Franklin IBE on the BLS12-381 curve as the KEM.

sources.quote: Seal uses a cryptographic primitive called *Identity-Based Encryption (IBE)* to encrypt stored data.
**KEM**: Boneh-Franklin IBE with the BLS12-381 curve.

entities.name: @mysten/seal TypeScript SDK
entities.description: TypeScript SDK on npm for integrating Seal encryption and decryption into applications.

sources.quote: The [TypeScript SDK](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mysten/seal) is available through npm.
The recommended way to encrypt and decrypt the data is to use the [Seal SDK](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mysten/seal).

entities.name: Move
entities.description: Smart contract language used to write application-specific Seal access policy packages on Sui.

sources.quote: A user can achieve this by implementing an application-specific Move package that must follow some Seal conventions.

entities.name: Threshold encryption
entities.description: A t-out-of-n scheme across multiple key servers providing Seal's privacy and liveness guarantees.

sources.quote: allowing users to realize `t-out-of-n` threshold encryption across `n` total key servers, where `t` is the minimum number required for decryption.
**Privacy** as long as fewer than `t` key servers are compromised.

entities.name: seal_approve
entities.description: Move entry functions that define custom authorization logic the key server evaluates before releasing keys.

sources.quote: Packages should define `seal_approve*` functions in their modules to control access to the keys associated with their identity namespace.
The `seal_approve*` functions are evaluated on full nodes using the `dry_run_transaction_block` RPC call.

entities.name: AWS KMS
entities.description: Web2 key management service that Seal explicitly contrasts itself against as a non-goal.

sources.quote: Seal is not a key management service like [AWS KMS](https://aws.amazon.com/kms/) or any other such Web2 service

sources.quote: To maximize privacy, Seal uses client-side encryption where the application or user is responsible to encrypt and decrypt the data.

topics.label: Access control on Sui via seal_approve & Move

sources.quote: such as `seal.mystenlabs.com`
secure sensitive data at rest on decentralized storage like [Walrus](https://docs.wal.app/)
Seal is a decentralized secrets management (DSM) service that relies on access control policies defined and validated on [Sui](https://docs.sui.io/concepts/components).
Seal uses a cryptographic primitive called *Identity-Based Encryption (IBE)* to encrypt stored data.
A Seal key server is a simple and lightweight service that validates access control on Sui and generates identity-based private keys for authorized users to decrypt encrypted data.
A user can achieve this by implementing an application-specific Move package that must follow some Seal conventions.
The [TypeScript SDK](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mysten/seal) is available through npm.
The `seal_approve*` functions are evaluated on full nodes using the `dry_run_transaction_block` RPC call.
Seal is not a key management service like [AWS KMS](https://aws.amazon.com/kms/) or any other such Web2 service
Key servers are off-chain services that validate onchain access policies and return derived secret keys to authorized users.
allowing users to realize `t-out-of-n` threshold encryption across `n` total key servers, where `t` is the minimum number required for decryption.

questions.question: What is Seal?
questions.answer: Seal is a decentralized secrets management (DSM) service that secures sensitive data at rest on decentralized storage like Walrus, using identity-based encryption with access controlled by onchain policies validated on Sui.

sources.quote: Seal enables identity-based encryption and decryption of sensitive data, with access controlled by onchain policies on Sui.
Seal is a decentralized secrets management (DSM) service that relies on access control policies defined and validated on [Sui](https://docs.sui.io/concepts/components).

questions.question: How does Seal control who can decrypt data?
questions.answer: Access is controlled by application-specific Move packages that define seal_approve* functions; off-chain key servers validate these onchain policies and return identity-based derived keys only to authorized users.

sources.quote: Packages should define `seal_approve*` functions in their modules to control access to the keys associated with their identity namespace.
Key servers are off-chain services that validate onchain access policies and return derived secret keys to authorized users.

questions.question: How does Seal avoid a single point of trust for decryption keys?
questions.answer: Seal supports t-out-of-n threshold encryption across multiple key servers, preserving privacy as long as fewer than t are compromised and liveness as long as at least t are available.

sources.quote: **Privacy** as long as fewer than `t` key servers are compromised.
**Liveness** as long as at least `t` key servers are available.

questions.question: What cryptography does Seal use?
questions.answer: Seal uses Boneh-Franklin IBE on the BLS12-381 curve as the KEM and AES-256-GCM or HMAC-based CTR mode as the DEM; AES-256-GCM is preferred for speed and HMAC-CTR is used when onchain decryption is required.

sources.quote: **KEM**: Boneh-Franklin IBE with the BLS12-381 curve.
Prefer `AES-256-GCM` for most use cases as it is faster. Use `HMAC-CTR` only when you require onchain decryption.

questions.question: How do developers integrate Seal into an application?
questions.answer: Developers use the @mysten/seal TypeScript SDK from npm, the recommended way to encrypt and decrypt data, configuring a SealClient with key server object IDs.

sources.quote: The recommended way to encrypt and decrypt the data is to use the [Seal SDK](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mysten/seal).
The [TypeScript SDK](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@mysten/seal) is available through npm.

questions.question: What storage and blockchain does Seal work with?
questions.answer: Seal secures data at rest on Walrus or any other onchain/offchain storage and leverages Sui for controlling access to the decryption keys.

sources.quote: Application developers and users can use Seal to secure sensitive data at rest on decentralized storage like [Walrus](https://docs.wal.app/) or any other onchain / offchain storage.
Seal leverages [Sui](https://docs.sui.io/concepts/components) for controlling access to the decryption keys

questions.question: What are the limitations of seal_approve functions?
questions.answer: seal_approve* functions are evaluated on full nodes via dry_run_transaction_block, must be side-effect free, and cannot modify onchain state, so they should not rely on frequently changing state or transaction ordering.

sources.quote: The `seal_approve*` functions are evaluated on full nodes using the `dry_run_transaction_block` RPC call.
`seal_approve*` functions must be side-effect free and cannot modify onchain state.

questions.question: Is Seal a Web2 key management service like AWS KMS?
questions.answer: No. Seal is explicitly not a key management service like AWS KMS or any other Web2 service, and should not be used to store highly sensitive data such as wallet keys or regulated personal data.

sources.quote: Seal is not a key management service like [AWS KMS](https://aws.amazon.com/kms/) or any other such Web2 service

questions.question: Where can you find the list of available Seal key servers?
questions.answer: Seal's documentation points to verified key servers at the Pricing page for a list of available key servers in different environments.

sources.quote: Refer to the [verified key servers](/Pricing#verified-key-servers) for a list of available key servers in different environments.
